chapter_summary: Two chapter fragments are presented. (1) Bhagavad Gita / Mahabharata Chapter 1 (Arjuna Visada Yoga, opening): the blind king Dhritarashtra asks his charioteer-bard Samjaya what his sons (the Kauravas) and the sons of Pandu (the Pandavas) did when assembled for war on the sacred field of Kurukshetra. Samjaya reports that Duryodhana, seeing the Pandava army drawn up in battle formation by Drupada's son (Dhrishtadyumna, disciple of Drona), approached his teacher Drona and enumerated the great warriors on both sides — Bhima, Arjuna, Yuyudhana (Satyaki), Virata, Drupada, Dhrishtaketu, Chekitana, Kashiraja, Purujit, Kuntibhoja, Shaibya, Yudhamanyu, Uttamauja, the son of Subhadra (Abhimanyu), and the sons of Draupadi on the Pandava side; and Bhishma, Karna, Kripa, Ashvatthama, Vikarna, and the son of Somadatta on his own. Duryodhana asks all to guard Bhishma; Bhishma roars and blows his conch, after which conches, drums and horns sound tumultuously, and Krishna (Madhava/Hrishikesha) and Arjuna (Pandava/Dhananjaya), seated in a chariot of white horses, blow their divine conches Panchajanya, Devadatta, and Bhima (Vrikodara) blows the great conch Paundra. (2) Narada Purana opening dialogue: the sages (rishis) ask Suta how Sanatkumara expounded all dharmas to Narada and where the two Brahma-knowers conversed. Suta narrates that the mind-born sons of Brahma — Sanaka, Sanandana, Sanatkumara, and Sanatana — desireless, egoless and celibate (urdhvaretas), devoted to Vishnu, set out for Brahma's assembly via Meru's peak and saw the Ganga (Vishnupadi). As they prepared to bathe, the divine sage Narada arrived, bowed, and praised Hari with many names (Narayana, Achyuta, Ananta, Vasudeva, Janardana, Krishna, Vishnu, Damodara, Keshava, and the avatara-names Rama, Narasimha, Vamana, Pradyumna, Sankarshana, Aniruddha). After bathing and purification, Narada poses questions on the nature of Bhagavan, the threefold karma that bears fruit, the marks of knowledge and austerity, and the worship of guests by which Vishnu is pleased; Shaunaka offers a hymn of salutation to the supreme Deva who is both with and without qualities.

story.title: On the Field of Dharma: Conches, and the Question of Hari
story.arc: Two openings of cosmic conversation. First, on Kurukshetra, Dhritarashtra's anxious question sets the Gita in motion; Duryodhana surveys both armies, names the warriors, and the conches of the Pandava side answer Bhishma's lion-roar with divine sound — the threshold before Krishna's teaching. Second, in the Narada Purana, the four eternal child-sages pause at the Ganga, Narada hymns Vishnu by his many names, and after purification poses the great questions — what is Bhagavan, what makes karma fruitful, what is true knowledge — opening the Purana's exposition of dharma.
story.timeline_note: Gita fragment: the instant before the Mahabharata war begins, on Kurukshetra. Narada fragment: a primordial, cyclical-time setting at the Ganga near Meru, framed as Suta's narration to the sages.
story.comic_potential: Duryodhana nervously over-listing his own side's heroes to his teacher Drona — name-dropping under pressure — while Bhishma cuts the tension by simply blowing a conch. And the four 'eternal children' sages being lectured-at by their famously restless younger brother Narada.

teachings.teaching: The battlefield is named 'the field of dharma' (dharmakshetra) — the place of righteous action is simultaneously the place of struggle; the war is fought on the ground of duty itself.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga framework the kurukshetra/dharmakshetra is the body (kshetra) of the practitioner: the inner battlefield where the discriminating self confronts the army of ego-born tendencies. The conflict is interior; the field of dharma is the prepared body in which sadhana is waged.

teachings.teaching: Both armies stand assembled with 'a desire for war' (yuyutsavah) — desire and conflict are the precondition that the coming teaching must transcend.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the contending hosts as the modifications (vrittis) of the mind; only when both 'sides' of restless desire are brought face to face within can stillness begin.

teachings.teaching: The blowing of the divine conches — Panchajanya, Devadatta, Paundra — is the sounding of primal vibration before the discourse of knowledge.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga the conch is the inner Om / anahata-nada heard at the threshold of deeper meditation; Krishna's conch (Hrishikesha, lord of the senses) sounding first signals that the Self governs the breath before wisdom dawns.

teachings.teaching: The four Kumaras are nirmama (free of mine-ness), nirahankara (egoless), and urdhvaretas (of upturned, conserved life-energy) — the marks of the perfected renunciate.
teachings.lens_note: Urdhvaretas is precisely the Kriya goal: the upward turning and conservation of the vital energy (retas/prana) through which consciousness ascends the spine; the Kumaras exemplify the redirected life-force that egolessness makes possible.

teachings.teaching: The Ganga is Vishnupadi — born from the foot of the Supreme — and its waters purify (vigatakalmashah) those who bathe with devotion.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads the Ganga as the descending stream of grace/prana flowing from the highest center (Vishnu's foot = the crown) down through the practitioner; inner bathing is the cleansing of karmic residue (kalmasha) by that descending current.

teachings.teaching: The one Hari is invoked under a multitude of names and avatara-forms (Narayana, Rama, Narasimha, Vamana, and the vyuhas Pradyumna, Sankarshana, Aniruddha) — the single reality wearing many forms.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the many names are the many aspects of the one inner Self realized at successive stages of meditation; the avataras and vyuhas map onto the unfolding of consciousness, not external history — devotion to the Name (japa) is the practical key.

teachings.teaching: Narada's core questions — how Hari is known, how threefold karma becomes fruitful, the marks of knowledge and austerity, how Vishnu is pleased by honoring a guest — set worship and right action as the path to the Supreme.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens makes 'knowing Hari' an experiential realization through Kriya rather than scriptural assent; karma becomes 'fruitful' only when offered without ego, and atithi-puja is read as honoring the divine present in all.

teachings.teaching: The Supreme is saluted as both saguna and nirguna — with qualities and without — beyond the highest, the indwelling Self (atmasamjna) and the form of the universe (vishvarupin).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma holds that Kriya leads the practitioner from the saguna (form, name, the conch-sound, the radiant Self) toward the nirguna (formless absolute); both are stages of one ascent, and the hymn's pairing of opposites marks the meditator's passage beyond all attributes.
